blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Piedmont Park Arts Festival**
📅 Saturday, August 15 and Sunday, August 16 | All day | Piedmont Park, Atlanta
🎟️ Free | [Learn More](https://www.atlantaparent.com/event/piedmont-park-arts-festival)

Mid-August, Piedmont Park fills up with artist demonstrations, live music, festival food, and a kids play area for two full days. The Piedmont Park Arts Festival is free to attend and easy to make a full day out of — especially if you combine it with brunch on the way in or dinner on the way back. It's a drive from East Cobb but the park is worth it when there's a reason to go. This one gives you a reason. Artists from across the region show up, the energy is good, and it never feels like the same thing twice.
scoring_notes: Solid annual arts festival with broad demographic appeal. Free entry removes friction. The drive to Piedmont Park is the main trade-off for an East Cobb audience, but the combination of arts, music, and food is a strong draw for this educated, higher-income demographic. Better fit for the empty-nester or adult segment than families with young kids. August dating makes this a useful forward-calendar item.

blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Atlanta Streets Alive**
📅 Sunday, May 31 | All day | Atlanta Beltline
🎟️ Free | [Learn More](https://www.atlantaparent.com/event/atlanta-streets-alive-2/2026-03-22)

Once or twice a year, Atlanta closes off a big stretch of road and hands it over to people on bikes, skateboards, and their own two feet. No cars, no traffic, just a long open corridor through the city to walk, ride, play, and explore. Atlanta Streets Alive is one of those events that sounds simple and turns out to be genuinely fun. The Beltline adds food, music, and art along the route. It's a great excuse to get into the city on a Sunday. Bikes, kids, dogs, strollers — all of it works. Free to join, zero barriers to entry.
scoring_notes: Twice-yearly cadence gives it reasonable uniqueness. Good fit for East Cobb families with active teens and adults. Slightly longer drive into Atlanta is the main friction point, but the Beltline is a well-known draw for this demographic. Free entry is a nice bonus though not the main selling point for this income level.

blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Georgia Renaissance Festival**
📅 Open weekends through May 31 | Fairburn
🎟️ Paid admission | [Get Tickets](https://www.atlantaparent.com/event/georgia-renaissance-festival/2026-04-11)

This is the last weekend stretch to catch the Georgia Renaissance Festival before it closes for the year. If you have never been, picture this: jousting knights, costume-clad merchants, turkey legs the size of your forearm, and performers doing things that make absolutely no sense and are completely worth watching. It runs through May 31, which means you are down to your final chances. It is about 45 minutes from East Cobb but worth every minute of the drive. Take the kids, take your parents, take anyone who could use a good weird afternoon outside.
scoring_notes: Annual event with a hard close date at end of May creates genuine urgency. Strong family and teen appeal, which fits East Cobb's mix of established families with older kids. Drive to Fairburn is a mild negative but the event is well-known and beloved in the Atlanta metro. Paid admission is a non-issue at $118K median income.

event_name: Flying Colors Butterfly Festival at Chattahoochee Nature Center
blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Flying Colors Butterfly Festival**
📅 Saturday, June 6 and Sunday, June 7 | All day | Chattahoochee Nature Center, Roswell
🎟️ Paid admission | [Get Tickets](https://www.atlantaparent.com/event/18th-annual-flying-colors-butterfly-festival)

The Chattahoochee Nature Center is basically in East Cobb's backyard, and every June they throw one of the most quietly spectacular festivals around. Live butterflies, pollinator experts, a Migration Game for kids, and live entertainment — all of it on the banks of the Chattahoochee. It's the kind of event that sounds educational but ends up being just a really good Saturday outside. Younger kids love it. Older kids tolerate it and then secretly enjoy it. Adults just want to sit by the river. Everybody wins.
scoring_notes: Chattahoochee Nature Center is a beloved local institution for this demographic. Roswell location is highly convenient. Annual event, but the butterfly festival specifically is a distinct, themed experience rather than a generic outing. Good fit for families with kids of any age, including teens who may have younger siblings. June dates give the newsletter useful forward planning value.

blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Alpharetta Arts Streetfest**
📅 Saturday, May 23 and Sunday, May 24 | All day | Wills Park, Alpharetta
🎟️ Free | [Learn More](https://www.atlantaparent.com/event/alpharetta-arts-streetfest)

This weekend, Wills Park turns into one big open-air gallery. Alpharetta Arts Streetfest brings together artisans, vendors, and street performers for two full days right across the Cobb county line. It's close, it's easy, and it costs nothing to walk through. Browse original art, pick up something handmade, or just enjoy a Saturday outside with good energy around you. Whether you go solo, bring a friend, or drag the family along, this one delivers. No tickets, no hassle — just show up.
scoring_notes: Strong fit for East Cobb's educated, higher-income audience who appreciates arts and local culture. Wills Park is a familiar, easy destination. Annual event but only comes once a year, two-day window creates mild urgency. Free entry removes all friction. Skews well toward both the established-family and empty-nester segments of the audience.

blurb:
⭐ **Featured Event: Southern-Fried Gaming Expo**
📅 July 31 through August 2 | Marriott Renaissance Waverly, Atlanta
🎟️ Paid admission | [Get Tickets](https://www.atlantaparent.com/event/southern-fried-gameroom-expo)

This one is for the parents who grew up in arcades and the teens who never got to. The Southern-Fried Gaming Expo takes over the Marriott Renaissance Waverly in Vinings, less than 20 minutes from East Cobb, with more than 300 arcade and pinball machines, retro consoles, tabletop games, and tournaments all weekend long. Everything is on free play once you're in, so you pay once and just go. It's loud, it's nostalgic, and it's genuinely one of the best ways to spend a weekend with a teenager who is hard to impress. Mark the calendar now — this one fills up.
scoring_notes: Exceptional demographic fit. The Waverly location is practically in East Cobb's backyard. The mix of nostalgia for Gen X parents (median age 42) and teen appeal matches this audience almost perfectly. Annual event with strong uniqueness. Free-play-after-entry model is a great value story for any income level. July dates are forward-looking but worth planting early.
